title: Permission error on named volume with docker-compose
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug


**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

When running `docker-compose up` with podman, a named volume has a permission error.
Using podman-compose with podman, or docker-compose with docker, works as expected.
Changing to short syntax with `z` label also works.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Clone [https://github.com/ypconstante/docker-elk/](https://github.com/ypconstante/docker-elk/) and checkout commit c96ad60fd8e12befa95b9ad82b1d3708969e5727

2. [Setup docker-compose to use rootless podman](https://fedoramagazine.org/use-docker-compose-with-podman-to-orchestrate-containers-on-fedora/)

3. Run `docker-compose up`

**Describe the results you received:**

```
elasticsearch_1  | ElasticsearchException[failed to bind service]; nested: FileSystemException[/usr/share/elasticsearch/data/nodes: Read-only file system];
elasticsearch_1  | Likely root cause: java.nio.file.FileSystemException: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data/nodes: Read-only file system
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

Service up and running

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.4.2
API Version:  3.4.2
Go Version:   go1.16.8
Built:        Sat Nov 13 21:16:48 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Can you include a `podman inspect` of a container using the volume? If it's mounted by path, I would expect that SELinux could be a problem.

I generated this two log files with some commands to compare the two executions.
The volume inspection has the same output for both tools.
When inspecting the containers, I saw that with docker-compose the volume has the RW flag as false, which seems to be the cause of the error I got. 

[podman.log](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/7619707/podman.log)
[docker.log](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/7619708/docker.log)
Potentially a volume option issue in the Docker API, then.
@jwhonce I think API logs would help here. Is your improved API logging at trace level, or debug level?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think this was answered on how to descover, since there has been no recent action, I am going to close.
